title: Notation & Matrix Operations
description: Flashcards covering matrix notation, special matrix types, transpose rules, multiplication conformability, and the column interpretation of Ax.

cards.back:
**Element at row $i$, column $j$**

Subscripts always read **row first, column second**.

*Usage: $a_{12}$ is row 1, column 2 — never row 2, column 1*

$$A = A^T \iff a_{ij} = a_{ji} \text{ for all } i,j$$

Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors (Spectral Theorem).

*Usage: covariance matrices, kernel matrices, Hessians of smooth losses*

A square matrix with nonzero entries only on the main diagonal:

$$D = \mathrm{diag}(d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n)$$

Represents **independent scaling** along each coordinate axis.

*Usage: $\Lambda$ in eigendecomposition; $\Sigma$ in SVD; isotropic Gaussian scale*

**Transpose reverses multiplication order:**

$$(AB)^T = B^T A^T$$

Generalises: $(ABC)^T = C^T B^T A^T$

*Usage: essential when differentiating matrix expressions; deriving the normal equations $A^T A \hat{x} = A^T b$*

**Conformability rule:** columns of $A$ must equal rows of $B$.

$$(m \times p)(p \times n) \to m \times n$$

The inner dimensions must match; the result has the outer dimensions.

*Usage: check shapes before every matmul — `A.shape[-1] == B.shape[0]`*

**No — matrix multiplication is not commutative.**

Even for square matrices of the same size, $AB \neq BA$ in general.

Exceptions: diagonal matrices commute; $AI = IA$; $A A^{-1} = I$.

*Usage: order matters in every chain of ops — attention, layer composition, backprop*

$$A\mathbf{x} = x_1 \mathbf{a}_1 + x_2 \mathbf{a}_2 + \cdots + x_n \mathbf{a}_n$$

The result is a **linear combination of the columns of $A$**, weighted by the entries of $\mathbf{x}$.

*Usage: a fully-connected layer computes $W\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$ — each output unit is a weighted sum of input features*

$$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b} = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i b_i \in \mathbb{R}$$

A scalar measuring alignment. Equals $\|\mathbf{a}\|\|\mathbf{b}\|\cos\theta$.

*Usage: attention scores $QK^T$ are batched inner products; similarity search; logits in a linear classifier*

$$\mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^T \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, \quad (\mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^T)_{ij} = a_i b_j$$

A rank-1 matrix — every row is a scalar multiple of $\mathbf{b}^T$.

*Usage: rank-1 updates; each term in the SVD $\sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$; value-weighted attention output*

$$(AB)C = A(BC)$$

Matrix multiplication is associative — you can re-bracket freely.

*Usage: choose the bracketing that minimises FLOPs; e.g., if $C$ is a column vector, compute $BC$ first to avoid forming the large matrix $AB$*

**Lower triangular $L$:** zeros above the diagonal.
**Upper triangular $U$:** zeros below the diagonal.

Products of triangular matrices of the same type stay triangular.

*Usage: LU factorisation $A = LU$; Cholesky $A = LL^T$ for PSD matrices; forward/back substitution*

cards.front: What does $\mathrm{tr}(A)$ equal, and what are its key properties?
cards.back:
$$\mathrm{tr}(A) = \sum_i a_{ii} = \sum_i \lambda_i$$

**Cyclic property:** $\mathrm{tr}(ABC) = \mathrm{tr}(BCA) = \mathrm{tr}(CAB)$

$\mathrm{tr}(A^T) = \mathrm{tr}(A)$; $\mathrm{tr}(A+B) = \mathrm{tr}(A)+\mathrm{tr}(B)$

*Usage: nuclear norm $\|A\|_* = \mathrm{tr}(\Sigma)$; Frobenius norm $\|A\|_F^2 = \mathrm{tr}(A^T A)$; gradient $\nabla_A \mathrm{tr}(A^T B) = B$*
